meta.title: Lev Architecture Session Decompilation
meta.description: A long-form visual decompilation of the entire architecture conversation: Poly compiled substrate, FlowMind/runtime layering, compiled-declaration spine, architecture ontology, OMX boundaries, research lane, and GPT vs Codex synthesis.

props.title: From PolyIR To Architecture Ontology
props.subtitle: The full arc of the conversation: freeze the Poly substrate, separate authoring from runtime, discover the compiled-declaration spine, then widen into a many-to-many architecture ontology without collapsing the system into one family.
props.meta: April 2026 · Poly, FlowMind, Stack, OMX, ontology, CDO, skills corpus

props.content: The session started as a FlowMind and Poly design problem, turned into a compiled-substrate problem, then escalated into a broader question: what recurring architecture shape is Lev actually discovering? The final result is two layers of truth. First, a concrete Poly implementation path with compiled IR, derived runtime, and proof-sequence-first execution. Second, a broader architecture ontology where **Compiled Declaration Architecture** is only one family inside a larger many-to-many map of planes, families, layers, and failure modes.

props.label: WHERE IT LANDED
props.title: The session’s stable conclusions
props.subtitle: This is the compressed answer before the long-form walkthrough.

props.label: SNAPSHOT
props.title: Current state
props.subtitle: What is frozen, what is optional, and what moved out of architecture debate.

props.title: ✅ Bottom line
props.content: Poly architecture is ready for implementation. The bigger discovery is not a replacement architecture for the whole repo, but a scoped family plus a richer ontology. The final split is: **Poly** as compiled capability contract, **FlowMind** as workflow/program layer, **lev exec / Stack** as runtimes, and **Architecture Ontology** as the analysis/reconstruction layer above them.

**Frozen implementation truth:** authored roots -> compileToPolyIR() -> deriveRegistryRuntime() -> CLI / Stack / FlowMind / surfaces. **Frozen runtime truth:** Stack owns steering first, FlowMind owns lowering later, receipts close runtime loops, topology stays outside Poly core. **Frozen ontology truth:** Compiled Declaration Architecture is real, but only as one family in a larger architecture ontology. Logger, the LevEvent spine, projections, and federation are all important, but they are not the same family.

props.label: FULL ARC
props.title: The conversation decompiled into phases
props.subtitle: This is the whole session, compressed into the decision sequence that actually changed the architecture.

items.title: FlowMind help-text problem becomes declaration-contract problem
items.description: The first correction was to stop treating the failure as generic lev help UX. The diagnosis moved down a layer: the invoked thing did not carry enough docs, validation, steering, and remediation semantics.

items.title: Poly source-of-truth strategy converges
items.description: The architecture settled on authored roots remaining canonical, compiled PolyIR as the semantic core, RegistryRuntime as a derived operational projection, and core/poly as consumer only.

items.title: poly-protocol and poly-compiler boundaries freeze
items.description: The repo converged on core/domain/src/poly-protocol/* for nouns, core/poly-compiler/* for compile and derive, and one-way shims for arm.ts and invoke-envelope.ts.

items.title: Static packet vs runtime packet split
items.description: The design separated static render packets derived from RegistryRuntime from Stack-emitted runtime authoring packets, preventing a second semantic system from appearing by accident.

items.title: Oracle contradiction cleanup
items.description: Small but concrete corrections landed: resume-safe Task linkage, union-by-id semantic profile merge, journey_projection, ordered Tick, stable non-file adapter IDs and digests, and tighter OMX boundary language.

items.title: Implementation planning, not more architecture
items.description: The architecture pass closed and became a proof-sequence-first implementation plan: poly-protocol, poly-compiler, fixtures, lev poly build|compile|derive, CLI proof, then Stack-backed runtime proof.

items.title: Meta-substrate discovery
items.description: A repo-wide sweep across core, plugins, and specs showed a recurring declaration -> lowering -> stable form -> runtime pattern, strongest in the FlowMind/Orchestration/Poly/Graph/UI spine.

items.title: CDO reframes the doctrine
items.description: The pattern survived adversarial testing, but only as a spine-scoped architecture family. The result: Compiled Declaration Architecture externally, compiled-declaration spine internally, lens discipline for governance.

items.title: Many-to-many ontology replaces one-family fit thinking
items.description: The conversation moved beyond 'does it fit the spine?' into planes, families, layer axes, and failure classes. Runtime became an axis that applies widely; architecture family membership stopped being treated as singular.

items.title: OMX/OMC boundary clarified
items.description: tmux panes, worktrees, worker counts, merge/retry/resume policies, and provider scheduling stayed in execution topology. Poly kept mailbox, task, report, heartbeat, summary, and receipt-shaped contracts at the adapter boundary.

items.title: Architecture ontology / ArchitectureIR question emerges
items.description: The GPT synthesis pushed one level higher: primitives, meta-structures, architecture families, and patch algebra. That reframed the missing layer as ArchitectureIR: a reconstruction and planning IR above subsystem-native IRs.

props.title: What changed the most
props.content: The deepest pivot was from feature design to ontology discipline. First the session froze a real compiler-shaped architecture for Poly. Then it realized that the same shape is only one member of a broader architectural ecology. That is why the later half of the conversation is less about commands and more about families, planes, projection discipline, and patch operators.

props.label: COMPILED STACK
props.title: How the layers actually line up
props.subtitle: FlowMind, Poly, Stack, and runtime surfaces are not the same thing. They rhyme structurally, but they own different jobs.

props.caption: One system, multiple aligned layers
props.content:
flowchart TD
  A[Authored roots\nconfig / DNA / stack catalogs / FlowMind roots] --> B[Poly compiler seam\ncompileToPolyIR() + deriveRegistryRuntime()]
  B --> C[PolyIR\ncompiled capability meaning]
  C --> D[RegistryRuntime\nderived runtime-safe projection]
  D --> E[CLI / Stack / MCP / HTTP / Mesh surfaces]
  C --> F[FlowMind lowering\nvia lowering_ref + semantic profiles]
  F --> G[lev exec / orchestration runtime]
  G --> H[Task -> WireFrame -> Tick -> Receipt]

rows.question: What is declared?
rows.owner: FlowMind roots, config, DNA, stack catalogs

rows.question: What does the system mean?
rows.artifact: PolyIR, PolyCard, PolyOp, Task, Tick, Receipt

rows.question: What do consumers need to read safely?
rows.warning: Not a second semantic source of truth

rows.question: How is the contract surfaced?
rows.owner: CLI, Stack, FlowMind lowering, MCP, HTTP, Mesh
rows.artifact: static packets, runtime packets, invoke path

props.content:
FlowMind writes meaning
Poly compiler freezes meaning
RegistryRuntime serves runtime-safe meaning
CLI and Stack render meaning
FlowMind lowering enacts meaning
Graph keeps truth
Receipts close the loop

props.label: ONTOLOGY SHIFT
props.title: The session eventually stopped comparing everything to one family
props.subtitle: This is where the conversation got broader: from one family note into a many-to-many architecture ontology.

props.caption: The many-to-many decompiler model
props.content:
flowchart TD
  A[Subsystem] --> B[Planes]
  A --> C[Architecture families]
  A --> D[Layer axes]
  A --> E[Failure modes]
  A --> F[Patch operators]
  B --> B1[control]
  B --> B2[execution]
  B --> B3[state]
  B --> B4[causality]
  B --> B5[projection]
  B --> B6[observability]
  C --> C1[Compiled Declaration Architecture]
  C --> C2[Event causality architecture]
  C --> C3[Projection architecture]
  C --> C4[Adapter runtime architecture]
  C --> C5[Observability architecture]
  D --> D1[declaration]
  D --> D2[lowering]
  D --> D3[stable form]
  D --> D4[runtime]
  D --> D5[federation]
  E --> E1[gap]
  E --> E2[drift]
  E --> E3[misalignment]
  F --> F1[extract unit]
  F --> F2[introduce boundary]
  F --> F3[introduce IR]
  F --> F4[split plane]
  F --> F5[add proof]

rows.note: Best demonstration that declaration -> lowering -> stable form -> runtime is real
Strong family member, but still implementation-drifted

rows.families: Event causality + adapter runtime + normalization pipeline
rows.note: Related structurally, but not the same family as the spine
The key correction: runtime is universal, family membership is selective
Orthogonal ontology, not just another compiled-declaration member
This is the new idea GPT pushed hardest: a reconstruction and patch-planning IR above subsystem-native IRs

props.title: ⚠️ The big correction
props.content: Runtime is not the deciding question. Nearly everything has a runtime. The useful question is: which planes, which family mix, which stable form, and which failure modes does the subsystem actually participate in?

**ArchitectureIR** only stays useful if it remains an analysis / reconstruction / planning IR. It collapses the moment it tries to replace Graph ontology, execution ontology, protocol ontology, or authored truth. The safe framing is: system reality -> architecture decompiler -> ArchitectureIR -> family classifier -> tradeoff atlas -> patch algebra -> refactor or extension plan.

props.label: ARTIFACT TRAIL
props.title: The conversation did not stay trapped in chat
props.subtitle: These are the durable outputs, pivots, and sidequests that came out of the thread.

rows.role: Compiled IR, compiler seam, runtime projection, proof sequence
First doctrine pass for declaration -> compiler -> IR -> runtime
Continuity and decision trace for the ontology pass
Runtime shell / adapter boundary prior art
Evidence for topology vs adapter-level contract split
External frameworks, GPT synthesis, timetravel results, approach catalog
Rendered visual artifacts for doctrine and ontology work

props.title: OMX / OMC sidequest
props.content: The OMX thread mattered because it proved a real runtime shell boundary. Bootstrap was not fully headless, but post-launch control was machine-drivable. The useful outcome was not 'adopt OMX semantics as core.' It was the sharper split: topology and swarm mechanics stay outside Poly core, while mailbox, event, summary, monitor, and receipt-shaped adapter contracts can still surface as runtime ops.

props.title: Skill / research lane
props.content: The architecture ontology conversation spawned a parallel research lane. That lane corrected a wrong move too: instead of inventing a new engineering hub, it fell back to the existing nested skills-db hub structure, preserved external framework excerpts and GPT synthesis as research artifacts, and kept active skill mutation out of the architecture kernel discussion.

props.label: BOTTOM COMPARISON
props.title: GPT’s analysis vs my analysis
props.subtitle: This is the comparison section you asked for at the bottom: where GPT was freakishly close, where it pushed the discussion forward, and where I would still tighten the framing.

props.title: GPT’s strongest contributions
props.content: GPT correctly identified the kernel as boring and small: capability / invoke / task / frame / receipt / gate / projection. It also pushed the conversation one level higher by articulating **ArchitectureIR**, architecture primitives, meta-structures, family signatures, and patch operators. The best parts were the primitive list, the meta-structures, the family table, and the patch algebra for refactor/extension.

props.title: My strongest corrections and constraints
props.content: I kept forcing scope discipline: do not widen Poly core, do not let topology semantics leak into substrate nouns, do not treat runtime as a family discriminator, and do not let ArchitectureIR replace subsystem-native ontologies. The key correction is that ArchitectureIR should be a **reconstruction / analysis / planning IR**, not a new root ontology or runtime substrate.

rows.codex: Agreed; keep future mesh/perimeter out of phase-1 substrate

rows.gpt: declaration -> lowering -> IR -> runtime -> optional federation
rows.codex: Agreed, but scoped; do not universalize without guardrails

rows.gpt: missing universal classifier + patch planner layer
rows.codex: Agreed, but only as reconstruction/planning IR

rows.gpt: adapter/runtime shell concerns sit outside kernel

rows.codex: strong signal; should survive into ontology note

props.content: GPT supplied the missing ambition. I supplied the brakes. The merged outcome is the useful one: keep the architecture reconstruction and patch-planning insight, but do not let it flatten the repo’s existing planes and ontologies.

props.label: 3 NEXT STEPS
props.title: What to do next
props.subtitle: Exactly three, and they follow the sequence implied by the whole conversation.

props.title: 1. Ship the Poly first slice
props.content: Stop expanding architecture and execute the frozen plan: create core/domain/src/poly-protocol/*, create core/poly-compiler/*, add fixture roots + goldens, wire lev poly build|compile|derive, and prove author_flowmind through the existing Stack path.

props.title: 2. Write ArchitectureIR v0
props.content: Create one focused design note for ArchitectureIR as an analysis / reconstruction / patch-planning IR. Keep it above Graph ontology, execution ontology, and protocol ontology. Start with primitives, meta-structures, family signatures, patch operators, and evidence_refs.

props.title: 3. Build the architecture decompiler
props.content: Run the first many-to-many classifier on a small set: FlowMind, Poly, Event Bus, Logger, and one runtime shell. Output planes, families, layer axes, failure modes, and patch candidates. Use that to replace the remaining spine-centric comparisons with a real architecture ontology workflow.
